coredata.dc:title: Macrophages-targeting mannosylated nanoparticles based on inulin for the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Qijuan
Arif, Muhammad
Chi, Zhe
Li, Guotao
Liu, Chen-Guang
coredata.dc:description: In the present experimental series, we have developed a novel nanocomposite to target activated macrophages in the colon with real time imaging and therapeutic capabilities. This binary nanocomposite was formed by the covalent conjugation of mannosylated NPs (Man-NPs) with carbon dots (CDs). Man-NPs were prepared using a self-assembly method based on mannosylated decamethylenediamine-grafted carboxymethyl inulin amphiphilic acid. While, the CDs were synthesized using a simple bottom-up process using citric acid monohydrate and diethylenetriamine, which were tightly bonded to the Man-NPs surface by carbodimide coupling. The resulting nanocomposite had a uniform size of 241.3 nm with a negative charge and a high drug casing density of 25.54 wt% and blue self-fluorescence were emitted. Whereas, in vitro observation of cellular uptake indicated the greater nanocomposite uptake in inflamed macrophage as compared to the untreated macrophage and mannose receptor-negative cell lines, 4T1 respectively. However, in vivo bio distribution exhibited a large number (60%) of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite accumulated in the inflamed colon of colitis mice. It should be noted that the novel nanocomposite, as macrophage-targeted drug delivery, could have promise for the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Carbon dots
Targeted drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading and in vitro release
Synthesis and characterization of CMI-DE-Man conjugates
Characterization of Man-NPs and unmodified NPs
Preparation and characterization of mannosylated nanocomposite (CDs/Man-NPs)
Drug loading and in vitro release

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key R & D project of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study is supported by Key R & D project of Shandong Province ( 2019GSF107031 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic scheme of CMI-DE conjugate, CMI-DE-Man conjugate, Man-NPs, CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite and apremilast loaded CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite.
1H NMR spectra of CMI in D2O and CMI-DE conjugate in DMSO (A). FTIR spectrums of CMI, CMI-DE conjugate and CMI-DE-Man conjugate (B).
TEM images of (A) Man-NPs and (B) CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite. Inset to figure shows the photographs of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite (marked by a rounded rectangle) and CDs (marked by circles).

(A) Uv–vis absorption of CDs (a) and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite (b) and fluorescence spectra of CDs (c) and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite (d). Inset to figure shows the photographs of CDs (e) and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite (f) in solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp light (right); (B) Fluorescence stability of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite in aqueous solution and serum; (C) In vitro release of apremilast from Man-NPs in simulated colon fluids (pH 6.8) at 37 °C; (D) Cell viability of Caco-2 cells treated with Man-NPs and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite at different concentrations for 24 h.
TNF-α expression level in untreated groups and LPS groups. Indicated values were mean ± S.D. of three experiments; *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001; ns, no significance.

CLSM images showing cell uptakes of activated macrophages, untreated macrophages and 4T1 cell. The cells were treated with CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite and unmodified nanocomposite (CDs/NPs), respectively. The differential interference contrast microscopy (DIC) of cells, nanocomposite (blue) and overlays of the two images. Bar = 20 μm.

Fluorescent quantitative analysis of cellular uptake of (A) CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite and CDs/NPs nanocomposite in LPS groups, (B) CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite before adding mannose (200 μmol/L,800 μmol/L) as a competitor, (C) CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite in LPS groups and without LPS groups, (D) CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite untreated macrophages and 4T1 cell; (E) Internalization mechanism of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite using various endocytic inhibitors including NaN3, chlorpromazine, genistein, Me-β-CD, wortmannin, and amiloride, (F) Distribution percentage of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite in different organs at 2 h, 4 h and 8 h. Indicated values were mean ± S.D. of three experiments; *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001; ns, no significance.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Indicated values were mean ± S.D. of three experiments.
Indicated values were mean ± S.D. of three experiments.

head.ce:title: Macrophages-targeting mannosylated nanoparticles based on inulin for the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Anesthesiology, Weifang Medical University, Baotong Road, Weifang 261053, Shandong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Anesthesiology, Weifang Medical University, Baotong Road, Weifang 261053, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Marine Life Science, Ocean University Of China, No.5 Yushan Road, Qingdao 266003, Shandong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Marine Life Science, Ocean University Of China, No.5 Yushan Road, Qingdao 266003, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Emergency Department, The Affiliated Hospital of Weifang Medical University, Yuhe Road, Weifang 261031, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Hospital of Weifang Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Emergency Department, The Affiliated Hospital of Weifang Medical University, Yuhe Road, Weifang 261031, Shandong, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present experimental series, we have developed a novel nanocomposite to target activated macrophages in the colon with real time imaging and therapeutic capabilities. This binary nanocomposite was formed by the covalent conjugation of mannosylated NPs (Man-NPs) with carbon dots (CDs). Man-NPs were prepared using a self-assembly method based on mannosylated decamethylenediamine-grafted carboxymethyl inulin amphiphilic acid. While, the CDs were synthesized using a simple bottom-up process using citric acid monohydrate and diethylenetriamine, which were tightly bonded to the Man-NPs surface by carbodimide coupling. The resulting nanocomposite had a uniform size of 241.3 nm with a negative charge and a high drug casing density of 25.54 wt% and blue self-fluorescence were emitted. Whereas, in vitro observation of cellular uptake indicated the greater nanocomposite uptake in inflamed macrophage as compared to the untreated macrophage and mannose receptor-negative cell lines, 4T1 respectively. However, in vivo bio distribution exhibited a large number (60%) of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite accumulated in the inflamed colon of colitis mice. It should be noted that the novel nanocomposite, as macrophage-targeted drug delivery, could have promise for the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a group of chronic disorders within the gastrointestinal tract, caused by dysregulated immune responses. Although the exact etiology of IBD is still unclear, however, studies have indicated the macrophages as primary antigen-presenting cells secreted a large number of cytokines (TNF-α) to regulate the migration and activation of other immune cells or non-immune cells and directly participate in the inflammatory response of intestinal mucosa [1,2]. The cumulative evidence showed that the abnormal innate immunity dominated by macrophages played a vital role in the pathogenesis of IBD [3]. Therefore, it was a promising therapeutic approach to target colonic macrophages for the treatment of IBD [4].

Recent studies indicated that the development of nanotechnology, a novel nanoplatforms have also exploited by the researchers using nanocomposites for drug delivery. Nanocomposites were comprised of natural species (mainly biopolymers) and nanofillers [5] of varied structure and morphology united at the nanometer scale, which can be widely used in the field of biomedicine. The nanocomposites could obtain multifunctional capabilities by conjugating two or more components, such as fluorescent molecules [6] and specific ligands moieties [7], with certain kind of NPs. These multifunctional composites can not achieve the therapy only, can also image inside the body by magnetic resonance imaging, fluorescence imaging, photoacoustic imaging, or computed tomography imaging, etc. [8]. Nigam et al. reported a drug delivery system with multifunction based on human serum albumin (HAS)-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite for pancreatic cancer bio-imaging and drug delivery [9]. Yuan et al. have reported a new delivery system based on gold-graphene nanocomposite for bio-imaging and delivery of doxorubicin to HepG2 cells in vitro [10]. Although significant progress has already documented in nano-drug delivery systems however, yet very few nanocomposites were available on colitis specific drug delivery.

Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as essential nanomaterials for their attractive properties in relatively high chemical and optical stabilities, superior water dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [11]. CDs are better substitutes of organic dyes, expensive fluorescent agents and semiconductor quantum dots. However, the fluorescence assay of CDs is limited for the lack of the nanoparticles' selectivity [12]. Inulin is a natural polysaccharide in diverse food and pharmaceutical applications. [13]. Unlike other polysaccharides, inulin could remain intact in the stomach's acidic environment as well as allowing degradation by inulinase in the colon. These unique properties made inulin more attractive colon-targeted drug delivery carrier [14,15].

Considering the excellent fluorescence properties of CDs and targeting abilities of the NPs, we developed a hypothesis on the combination of components might be a unique drug delivery system with real time imaging and therapeutic capabilities. Mannosylated nanoparticles (Man-NPs) were prepared by self-assembly method based on mannosylated decamethylenediamine-grafted-carboxymethyl inulin amphiphile in aqueous media. Carboxymethyl inulin (CMI) was biocompatible, biodegradable and non-toxic polysaccharide, bearing carboxyl groups in the side chain of inulin [16]. 1, 10-Decamethylenediamine (DE) was chosen as the hydrophobic segment partly due to the hydrophobic nature of a long aliphatic chain and its diamine groups. One amine could react with carboxyl groups of CMI; another amine-terminated alkyl chain was used to conjugate with mannose via Schiff's base. The amphiphile CMI-DE-Man derivatives could form self-aggregated nanoparticles in aqueous media. Mannose, as a most used ligand, could have high affinity with mannose receptor (MR) exclusively expressed on the surface of macrophages [17]. The mannosylated nanoparticles (Man-NPs) were prepared by the introduction of mannose moieties to CMI-DE conjugate. Man-NPs could trigger the preferential cell uptake via receptor-mediated internalization by MR on the surface of macrophages and avoid non-target cell uptakes [18]. CDs were synthesized by a simple bottom-up method using citric acid monohydrate and diethylenetriamine [19]. The CDs/Man nanocomposite was then fabricated by covalent conjugation of Man-NPs with CDs via carbodimide coupling. Recently, in contrast to the direct anti-tumor necrosis factor (TNF) agents, the novel phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4) inhibitors apremilast with better safety have been introduced as new modulators of intracellular signals and gene transcription for the treatment of IBD [20]. Apremilast have been already approved for the treatment of psoriasis, and psoriatic arthritis in the U.S.A., Europe and a phase 2 study in ulcerative colitis patients were ongoing. However, the marketed apremilast immediate-release formulation (APM IR) tablets showed low bioavailability after oral administration due to poor water solubility. Therefore, we explored apremilast as a model drug to analyze the potential of nanocomposite as a targeted drug delivery vehicle for IBD.

In the present study, we have evaluated the physicochemical properties (e.g., hydrodynamic diameters, drug loading capability) and the optical properties of the nanocomposite. Additionally, we have assessed the selective and specific targeting effect of nanocomposite on activated macrophages in vitro and bio-distribution in dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced colitis mice model in vivo respectively.

Inulin was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), with an average degree of polymerization (DP) = 20–30. Apremilast was purchased from Beijing Mesochem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). D-(+)-mannose, 1, 10-Decamethylenediamine (DE), and Wortmannin were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Citric acid monohydrate and Diethylenetriamine were purchased from Shanghai Medpep Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino)-propyl] carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), Methylated-β-cyclodextrin (Me-β-CD), Lipopolysaccharides (LPS, obtained from Escherichia coli), amiloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Genistein, Chlorpromazine and Sodium azide (NaN3) were obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and all other reagents were analytical grade and used as received.

In the previous report, we synthesized carboxymethyl inulin (abbreviated as CMI) by carboxymethylation of inulin, bearing carboxyl groups in the side chain [21]. The degree of substitution (DS) was 0.97 analyzed by spectrophotometric titration. To further synthesize the CMI-DE conjugate, the carboxylate groups located on CMI reacted with the primary amine of 1, 10-Decamethylenediamine (DE) by an acid amide bond. First, 3.4 g of CMI was dissolved in 10 mL distilled water and then converted into its free acid form by hydrochloric acid. Then the molar equivalence of NHS and EDC (the molar ratio of 1:1) was added to the active carboxyl group. After 3 h mixing, DE ethyl alcohol solution (10 mL) was added to the solution at a 3:1 M equivalence (amino groups of DE: carboxyl groups of CMI) and stirred at room temperature. The pH was again adjusted at 6.5 with phosphate buffer. The CMI-DE conjugate was subsequently characterized by FTIR spectroscopy and 1H NMR spectroscopy. After lyophilization, the mannosylated polymer CMI-DE-Man conjugates was subsequently synthesized by a modified method as previously described. Briefly, D-(+) mannose (0.8 mM) was dissolved in sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0; 0.1 M) and subsequently added to the CMI-DE conjugates solution (0.34 g/mL). The mixture was allowed to be continuously stirred at ambient temperature for 2 days to ensure the completion of reaction. The obtained products were subjected to extensive dialysis (dialysis bag; MWCO 500–1000 Da) against double distilled water for 30 min to remove free mannose, followed by lyophilization. The resulting conjugates were subsequently characterized by FTIR spectroscopy and 1H NMR spectroscopy. A colorimetric assay calculated the graft rate of mannose for reducing sugars.

After incubation for 24 h, the CMI-DE-Man conjugates were spontaneously self-assembled in aqueous solution by a probe-type sonifier (Sonics Ultrasonic Processor, VC750) in an ice bath to facilitate the formation of the hydrophobic domain. The resultant nanoparticles solutions were filtered through a filter (0.8 μmol pore size) to remove dust. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a Zetasizer (Malvern Co., UK) was used to determine the size and distribution of these particles.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized according to a reported method. Briefly, citric acid monohydrate (1.2 g) and diethylenetriamine (600 μL) were added into 20 mL of ultrapure water. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon equipped stainless steel auto-clave (30 mL), which was placed in a drying oven followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h. When cooled at room temperature, the products were concentrated by rotary evaporation, followed by purification with ethanol for three times.

For direct visualization of the cellular uptake and enhanced functionality, CDs were introduced onto Man-NPs forming the mannosylated nanocomposite (CDs/Man-NPs). The unmodified nanocomposite (CDs/NPs) were prepared based on CDs and nanoparticles (NPs) without mannose modification. CDs were added on the surface of the nanoparticles by well-established carbodiimide chemistry [22]. The EDC/NHS were added to 20 mL CDs solution and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min at 400 rpm. After that, 3.4 g Man-NPs were added to the reaction mixture for two days. After lyophilization, the pale-yellow product was obtained. The mannosylated nanocomposite was subsequently prepared at the same ultrasonic conditions as Man-NPs. The whole process was done in the dark. The fluorescence microplate reader (FLx800B, Bio-Tek, USA) was calibrated with standard solutions of CDs and labelling efficiency was calculated by dividing the weight of CDs with the predetermined amount of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite. The UV–vis absorption of CDs and the nanocomposite were investigated by the UV–visible spectrophotometer (TU-1810, Purkinje, China) and the fluorescence spectrum (λEx = 362 nm, λEm = 450 nm) was recorded by Fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and in vitro release
ce:section.ce:para:
The drug loading capacity was detected in Man-NPs and in the nanocomposite; CDs which could not encapsulate the hydrophobic drugs. In reverse, CDs also could not affect the interactions between Man-NPs and drug as shown in Fig. S1. The CDs/Man-NPs and Man-NPs were used to encapsulate apremilast. Briefly, 240 mg of CDs/Man-NPs and Man-NPs were dissolved in 6 mL DI water, followed by the addition of apremilast-DMSO solution (1 mg/mL). The mixture was then treated at the same ultrasound condition, and the apremilast loaded CDs/Man-NPs and Man-NPs was obtained. The encapsulation efficiency (EE) and drug loading efficiency (LE) of apremilast was determined by HPLC using a ZORBAX SB-Aq column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm, Agilent, USA), and connecting with a UV detector (Agilent Technologies, USA) at 230 nm. The column temperature was 30 °C. The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 0.1% formic acid solution (isocratic wash off) (94:6, v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The method was linear (r2 = 0.9910) over a range of 50 μg/mL to 750 μg/mL.
EE = W /
W 0
× 100 %
where W was the mass of drug in the nanoparticles and W0 was the mass of the drug-loaded nanoparticles.

The drug loading capacity was detected in Man-NPs and in the nanocomposite; CDs which could not encapsulate the hydrophobic drugs. In reverse, CDs also could not affect the interactions between Man-NPs and drug as shown in Fig. S1. The CDs/Man-NPs and Man-NPs were used to encapsulate apremilast. Briefly, 240 mg of CDs/Man-NPs and Man-NPs were dissolved in 6 mL DI water, followed by the addition of apremilast-DMSO solution (1 mg/mL). The mixture was then treated at the same ultrasound condition, and the apremilast loaded CDs/Man-NPs and Man-NPs was obtained. The encapsulation efficiency (EE) and drug loading efficiency (LE) of apremilast was determined by HPLC using a ZORBAX SB-Aq column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm, Agilent, USA), and connecting with a UV detector (Agilent Technologies, USA) at 230 nm. The column temperature was 30 °C. The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 0.1% formic acid solution (isocratic wash off) (94:6, v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The method was linear (r2 = 0.9910) over a range of 50 μg/mL to 750 μg/mL.
EE = W /
W 0
× 100 %
where W was the mass of drug in the nanoparticles and W0 was the mass of the drug-loaded nanoparticles.

The release of apremilast was conducted under simulated colon fluid (SCF) with pH 6.8 containing 0.5% w/v Tween® 80 at 37 °C. The apremilast-loaded CDs/Man-NPs and Man-NPs were dissolved in a 6 mL medium and immediately transferred to a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off: 500-1000 Da). At designated time intervals, 200 μL of release medium was taken out and then centrifuged (TGL-20 M, Xiangyi, China) at 12,960 ×g for 20 min. The cumulative drug release was calculated by HPLC measurement and contrasted with apremilast suspension.

Caco-2 cells were seeded on 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h. The nanocomposite and Man-NPs at the desired concentration were respectively added to each well for 24 h, after which methyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay was used. Sodium deoxycholate (1 mg/mL) was a positive control, while untreated cells were served as 100% cell viability. For each sample, tests were performed in quadruplicate.
Raw 264.7 cells and Murine breast carcinoma 4T1 cells were purchased from the Shanghai Cell line bank and maintained in RPMI-1640 containing 10% FBS (Gibco, USA).

Cellular uptakes and distributions of CDs/Man-NPs and CDs/NPs were evaluated by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (Revolve FL, Echo, USA). Raw 264.7 cells were seeded on glass slides coated with gelatin (1% w/v) at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well in 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Half of the Raw 264.7 cells were exposed to the LPS (100 ng/mL) to obtain an inflamed model as described earlier, while the other half of Raw 264.7 cells were used as an untreated control. Culture media was replaced by 2 mL of serum-free culture medium containing CDs/Man-NPs and CDs/NPs, respectively, followed by incubation for 4 h at 37 °C. After that, the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4), fixed with a 4% paraformaldehyde solution and then observed. For competitive inhibition study of CDs/NPs, free mannose at concentrations 10 times greater than the mannose content of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite was additionally added to the serum-free medium containing CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite, and intercellular localization was investigated over inflamed Raw 264.7 cells. 4T1 cells were untreated as a negative control.

Furthermore, fluorescence microplate reader was used to quantify the cellular uptake of the nanocomposite as follows. Cells with a density of 5 × 104 cells/well in 6-well plates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Inflamed and untreated Raw 264.7 cells were prepared as described above. After incubation for 4 h at 37 °C with the serum-free culture medium containing CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite, the cells were dissolved in 50 μL of 0.5% Triton X-100 solution. After that, the fluorescence intensity of each well was measured at the excitation wavelength (Ex) 362 nm and the emission wavelength (Em) 450 nm. To confirm mannose receptor-mediated internalization, free mannose at concentrations 10 times greater than the mannose content of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite was added to the serum-free medium before CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite. 4T1 cells were untreated as a negative control. To quantify the fluorescence intensity of the cells was determined directly by dissolving a specified amount of the CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite in distilled water.

To explore the further cellular uptake mechanism, Raw 264.7 cells were first treated with various endocytic inhibitors for 30 min as follows: 10 mmol/L NaN3, 10 μg/L chlorpromazine, 200 μg/mL genistein, 50 nmol/L wortmannin, 50 μmol/L Me-β-CD and 500 μmol/L amiloride. The mannosylated nanocomposite was then added, and fluorescence microplate reader determined the fluorescence intensity in each group. The fluorescence intensity of the untreated cells without endocytic inhibitors was were served as 100%.

Colitis was induced by oral administration 5% (w/v) DSS in tap water ad libitum for 7 days. After induction of colitis, all the male BALB/c mice, aged between 8 and 12 weeks, were given a gavage of 150 μL CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite. At 2, 4 and 8 h after oral administration, blood was collected from the heart under anesthetization, and the fresh tissues (liver, spleen, lung, kidney, small intestine, and colon) were isolated immediately, weighed, and homogenized on ice. The resulting homogenates were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, the amount of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite in the supernatant, as well as plasma, was quantified by a fluorescence microplate reader at excitation and emission wavelengths of 362 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The results were expressed as a percentage of the total amount of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite.

Statistical analysis was performed using Student's t-test. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey's tests was used to analyze the differences between groups. Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (S.D.). Statistical significance was represented by *p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CMI-DE-Man conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The amphiphilic copolymer was obtained by hydrophobically modified carboxymethyl inulin (CMI). 1,10-Decamethylenediamine (DE) was introduced as a hydrophobic segment to the backbone of CMI, a hydrophilic polymer, through an acid amide bond, as shown in Fig. 1 . The obtained CMI-DE conjugate could self-assemble into nanoparticles in aqueous solution due to the long aliphatic side chains. DE was selected as a hydrophobic agent not only due to its long aliphatic chain also for its diamine groups. One amine could react with carboxyl groups of CMI, and the other amine-terminated alkyl chain was used to conjugate with the other substance. The 1H NMR spectra data (Fig. 2A) showed signals at 3.0–4.5 ppm were assigned to the protons of carboxymethyl inulin [23]. The appearance of the new signals in the regions of 1.29 ppm and 2.77 ppm in CMI-DE conjugate (in DMSO) was the evidence of aliphatic peaks of DE [24]. However, these results indicated that the CMI-DE conjugate was obtained. Meanwhile, two sharp peaks corresponding to solvent were found at 2.6 ppm and 4.6 ppm. The degree of substitution (DS) was calculated by comparing the relative intensity ratio of aliphatic chain protons at 1.29 ppm to sugar protons at 3.09 ppm. The degree of DE substitution was about 41%. To functionalize this amphiphilic polymer, mannose was grafted to the CMI-DE conjugated via Schiff's base. After opening the ring of mannose in acid solution, the exposed aldehyde group subsequently reacted with a free amine of the CMI-DE conjugated [25]. This led to the formation of Schiff's base (–NCH–). The inferences of FITR analysis (Fig. 2B) indicated two strong peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 1422 cm−1 assigned to the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching of a COO− group in CMI [26]. When grafted with DE, several bands appeared at 1703.1 cm−1, and 3200–3400 cm−1 could be assigned to –CN stretching of amide II [27] and NH bending of a primary amine in CMI-DE conjugate. After mannosylation, the presence of broad OH stretching at 3200–3500 cm−1 assigned to mannose confirmed the synthesis of CMI-DE-Man conjugate [25]. However, the Ninhydrin colorimetry assay showed the graft rate of mannose was 5%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Man-NPs and unmodified NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The amphiphilic polymer could self-assemble into nanoparticles in aqueous solution, and the nanoparticles would be more functional after modification with mannose [18]. Zetasizer measured the average size and PDI. Our findings showed the average particle size of Man-NPs was 241.3 ± 0.7 nm which was greater than unmodified NPs (204.5 ± 0.3 nm) (Table 1 ). Hence, the possible reason could be that mannose as soluble sugar present on the surface of nanoparticles increased the size of Man-NPs. Thus, the zeta potential of all NPs was observed to be negative charge possibly due to the rich carboxyl groups distributed on the surface of the nanoparticles (Table 1) [26]. The negatively charged surface of Man-NPs might be beneficial for passive targeting of the inflamed intestinal areas [28]. However, the zeta potential of Man-NPs lowered to −11.7 ± 0.6 mV as compared to the unmodified NPs. The possible reason was the positively charged free amino groups were shielded due to mannose conjugation [25]. The Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies showed the Man-NPs had the smooth surface and spherical shape (Fig. 3A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of mannosylated nanocomposite (CDs/Man-NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: To directly visualize the location of the nanoparticles, the mannosylated nanocomposite (CDs/Man-NPs) were prepared by carbon dots (CDs) and mannosylated nanoparticles (Man-NPs). This novel system could accomplish two tasks at once; one task about drug delivery carriers, and the other was self-fluorescence to reduce the potential toxicity. The unmodified nanocomposite (CDs/NPs) were prepared based on CDs and unmodified nanoparticles (NPs) without mannose modifiation. The fabrication of CDs/Man-NPs was through the esterification reaction between the –COOH of CDs and –OH of Man-NPs [29,30]. Here, the labelling efficiency of CDs was about 21.6% calculated by standard solutions of CDs. TEM studies (Fig. 3B) indicated the CDs/Man-NPs have spherical shapes, and the distributions of CDs in CDs/Man-NPs could be observed. Whereas, the large particles with a diameter of 247.2 nm were identified as CDs/Man-NPs. In comparison, the surrounding smaller particles with an average size of 13–15 nm were considered as CDs (marked by circles). It should be noted that the CDs and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite had a clear absorption peak at 362 nm attributed to n-π* transition of CO bonds (Fig. 4A). The CDs and CDs/Man-NPs emitted strong blue fluorescence when excited with a UV lamp (365 nm). The maximum emission wavelength of CDs was at 450 nm under an excitation wavelength of 362 nm. After binding to the nanoparticles, the maximum emission wavelength of CDs/Man-NPs blue-shifted for 6.0 nm with a severe development of fluorescence intensity. This implied that the interaction between the hydroxyl groups of Man-NPs and carboxyl groups of CDs further decreased the surface defects of CDs resulting in the enhancement of fluorescence intensity of CDs/Man-NPs [31]. In brief, the fluorescence stability of the CDs/Man-NPs was investigated under different conditions when excited at the maximum excitation wavelength for a long time. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite remained nearly unchanged, no matter in solution or in the cell culture medium, which indicated the CDs/Man-NPs have excellent resistance to photobleaching (Fig. 4B). These results showed that the as-prepared CDs/Man-NPs had the suitable luminescent property, which provide the possibility for bio-imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and in vitro release
ce:section.ce:para: In the hydrophobic drug model, apremilast was entrapped into the hydrophobic microdomain of nanoparticles by hydrophobic interactions [32]. Whereas, on account of the optical property of CDs in the nanocomposite could affect the detection of free apremilast in the supernatant. Therefore, singleton Man-NPs was used to encapsulate apremilast without combining with CDs and CDs could not affect the interactions between nanoparticles and drug as shown in Fig. S1. As our findings showed that the Man-NPs had a particular ability to encapsulate apremilast (Table 2). Based on our previous experiences, the encapsulation efficiency (EE) was mutually related to the feed ratio of polymer and drug. However, with increase of the drug feed ratio, the encapsulation efficiency (EE) and the drug loading efficiency (LE) of nanoparticles increased from 11.22 wt% to 44.16 wt% and from 3.74 wt% to 16.36 wt%, respectively.

In the hydrophobic drug model, apremilast was entrapped into the hydrophobic microdomain of nanoparticles by hydrophobic interactions [32]. Whereas, on account of the optical property of CDs in the nanocomposite could affect the detection of free apremilast in the supernatant. Therefore, singleton Man-NPs was used to encapsulate apremilast without combining with CDs and CDs could not affect the interactions between nanoparticles and drug as shown in Fig. S1. As our findings showed that the Man-NPs had a particular ability to encapsulate apremilast (Table 2). Based on our previous experiences, the encapsulation efficiency (EE) was mutually related to the feed ratio of polymer and drug. However, with increase of the drug feed ratio, the encapsulation efficiency (EE) and the drug loading efficiency (LE) of nanoparticles increased from 11.22 wt% to 44.16 wt% and from 3.74 wt% to 16.36 wt%, respectively.

Meanwhile, the particle size of drug-loaded Man-NPs also increased from 265.7 nm to 363.4 nm depending on the drug content. This may be due to the fact that apremilast, as a hydrophobic drug, was incorporated within the hydrophobic microdomains of the copolymers via hydrophobic interactions. Higher the concentration of the drug, the expansion of hydrophobic core will result in more increase of the particle size [33]. It could be directly seen that the nanospheres containing 25.42 wt% apremilast had a small size of 323.9 nm with a relatively low polydispersity index (PDI) value of 0.221, which suggested higher stability than others. The stability was of great significance for avoiding drug release before nanospheres reaching the targeted location [15]. Thus, this formulation was choosing to strength the information for further studies.

To deliver the apremilast loaded CDs/Man-NPs to the colonic lumen, we encapsulated them into the enteric-coated capsule as a potential oral drug delivery system. Enteric-coated capsule loaded with the freeze-dried NPs could be used for oral delivery of the nanoparticles to the targeted areas [34]. Thus, the release of NPs from enteric-coated capsule would occur mostly in the colonic lumen rather than other parts of the gastrointestinal tract, such as the stomach or small intestine. The dialysis method was used to study the drug release behaviour of nanoparticles in simulated colon fluids (pH 6.8) at 37 °C, as shown in Fig. 4C. There was a relatively high initial burst release (about 68.12 wt% within 2 h) of apremilast suspension and almost all drugs released within 12 h, these findings were consistent with previous reports [35]. However, approximately 75.19 wt% drugs from nanoparticles were slowly released and continued for 12 h in simulated intestinal fluid. All these findings indicated that, the drug-loaded Man-NPs could effectively deliver the drug to the colon with slowly release as compared to the drug suspensions. The reason might be related to the stability of structure of the nanoparticle. Inulin was a neutral polysaccharide, after carboxymethylation, CMI became a negatively charged polysaccharide due to the presence of a carboxyl group on the backbone [26]. In the neutral and alkalic environment, the free carboxylic acid groups (pKa = 2.0–4.0) were ionized to generate electrostatic repulsion and induce to form the loose nanoparticle structure. Some drugs were exposed to the physiological buffer solutions and slowly released under a driven concentration gradient.

A series of experiments was conducted to investigate the potential toxicity of Man-NPs and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite for oral drug deliveries. The methyl tetrazolium (MTT) method was used to analyze the cytotoxicity of all samples in the Caco-2 cell line (Fig. 4D). The results showed cell viabilities treated with Man-NPs and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite were both maintained over 80% under the various sample concentrations for 24 h. These findings suggested that Man-NPs and CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite had excellent biocompatibility and innocuous, therefore they would be suitable for in vivo usage.

Generally, nanoparticles were internalized into the cells through endocytosis, then absorbed and utilized by lysosomes and the whole process was very complicated [40]. Ligand internalization was just one form of endocytosis, involving many factors. Therefore, the internalization pathways of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite were studied by various endocytosis inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 7E, the cellular uptake level of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite substantially decreased to 16.32%, after treated with metabolic energy inhibitor NaN3, indicating the internalization pathways of CDs/Man-NPs dependent on energy in contrast with the control group. The similar phenomenon was also observed in Me-β-CD and genistein treated groups (10.53% and 32.14%, respectively), suggesting the cellular uptake of CDs/Man-NPs via caveolin-dependent endocytosis. Wortmannin and amiloride, as the micropinocytosis inhibitors, could inhibit the uptake level of nanoparticles, demonstrating the involvement of macropinocytosis pathways [41]. While, there was a negligible decline in the chlorpromazine treated group, suggesting that the nanoparticle uptake was not associated with clathrin-mediated endocytosis. These results indicated that the mannose-MR recognition did not notably alter their internalization mechanism in macrophages. The internalization of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite via caveolae-mediated endocytosis or macropinocytosis could be directly transported to the Golgi and/or endoplasmic reticulum to trigger cytoplasmic drug release rather than experiencing endosomal entrapment and lysosomal degradation following clathrin-mediated endocytosis [41].

Generally, after absorption, drug and/or drug-loaded nanocarrier could be included in cytoplasmic vesicles or diffused into the cytoplasm and discharged in the serosal spaces to gain access to the mesenteric lymph or blood [42]. The utilization of optical property on CDs/Man-NPs as the tracking probe could improve the visualization of particles transport and cell response. In this study, a large number (up to 60%) of CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite accumulated in inflamed colon, followed by the small intestine (about 20%), liver, blood, kidneys in dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced colitis mice model (Fig. 7F). However, it was negligible in the lung and spleen. The distribution of CDs/Man-NPs in the colon and small intestine were remarkably increased for the first 4 h then slightly decreased in following 4 h. This might be due to the CDs/Man-NPs with the negative charge facilitating its adhesion to the positively charged inflamed colon epithelium and further endocytosis by active macrophages in lamina propria, after releasing from the enteric-coated capsule. The following slight decrease could be owed to diarrhoea and hematochezia that make some loss of nanocomposite. The distribution levels in the plasma and liver were remarkably increased with time extension. These results demonstrated that CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite could selectively target the inflamed colon rather than entering systemic circulation via intestinal absorption [37].

In summary, a binary nanocomposite was successfully prepared based on carbon dots (CDs) and mannosylated NPs (Man-NPs) for its successful implementation for inflamed macrophages specific drug delivery. This novel nanoplatform could accomplish two tasks at once; one task about targeted drug delivery carriers, having the high drug envelopment rate and specific targeting to active macrophages in the colitis model, the other task was self-fluorescence to track the location of nanoparticles and perform cell imaging, with the potentially low toxicity. The inferences showed that the well-formed mannosylated nanocomposite with a negative charge have a desirable size distribution with high drug loading capacity. The mannosylated nanocomposite exhibited higher optical property under UV light as compared to CDs. In vitro, cellular uptake study indicated mannosylated nanocomposite have good macrophage-targeting ability and greater uptake in inflamed macrophage as compared to the untreated macrophage and mannose receptor-negative cell lines, 4T1 cell. In vivo bio-distribution revealed that CDs/Man-NPs nanocomposite could selectively target to the inflamed colon, avoiding the non-targeted tissue absorption. The novel multifunction mannosylated nanocomposite with multifunction, as macrophage-targeted drug delivery, is a promising therapeutic approach in the treatment of IBD.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Characterization of apremilast loaded CDs/Man–NPs. In vitro release of apremilast from CDs/Man-NPs in simulated stomach fluid (SGF) with pH 1.2, simulated colon fluid (SCF) with pH 6.8. Indicated values were mean ± S.D. of three experiments.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.12.094.
